html:
Nanjing Well Pharmaceutical Group (SSE:603351): the better thesis is specialty materials, not just excipients

Nanjing Well looks like a sleepy A-share pharma supplier, but the business is more interesting: a high-margin regulated excipient franchise plus a synthetic base-oil platform aimed at refrigeration, wind, EV drivetrains, robotics, and data-center liquid cooling. At the latest verifiable quote I found, CNY 27.80 on May 15, 2026, the market cap is about CNY 3.8 billion. That is only about 25x trailing EPS, around 22x my 2026 base EPS, with a 2% plus dividend yield and a balance sheet that is not stretched.

The prior version of the thesis was directionally right that pharma excipients are the quality asset, but too clean. Well is not about excipients quickly overtaking the whole company. In 2025, synthetic lubricating base oils still produced roughly CNY 900 million of sales, while excipients produced roughly CNY 350 million, based on disclosed operating data from Tonghuashun. The actual variant perception is different: the “chemical” segment is itself migrating from cyclical industrial oils toward high-specification specialty fluids, while the pharma segment is being protected by higher GMP and registration barriers.

The latest numbers look boring on the surface. The 2025 annual report showed revenue up 2.95%, net profit up 3.11%, and operating cash flow up 7.42% to CNY 238 million. The Q1 2026 report showed revenue up only 1.88% and net profit up 2.86%. But the hidden detail is volume versus price. In Q1, excipient revenue rose modestly to CNY 87.6 million while average excipient selling price fell, and synthetic base-oil revenue rose to CNY 248 million while lube prices also fell. Volumes are better than reported revenue growth suggests. Gross margin rose to 27.48% in Q1 2026 despite price pressure, which is the early evidence that mix, utilization, and raw-material spreads are starting to work.

The regulatory setup is unusually favorable. China’s NMPA excipient and packaging-material GMP appendices became effective January 1, 2026, and regulators are pushing manufacturers toward stronger quality systems. That is bad for small under-invested excipient producers and good for Well, which has already built a GMP-style green pharmaceutical-materials base and had at least 90 CDE-disclosed raw material and excipient registrations by mid-2025, including injectable-route products. The market is not paying much for that barrier because 603351 still screens as a low-growth chemical/pharma-small-cap hybrid.

The lube side is also being misread. This is not just passenger-car engine oil. The company is selling synthetic base oils into refrigeration compressors, wind power, electric-drive oils, thermal-management fluids, and environmentally acceptable lubricants. Its 5,800-ton POE ester expansion has moved into production, while a 30,000-ton synthetic new-materials project remains the larger embedded option. Retail already tags the stock with liquid-cooling and small-cap growth concepts, but the market has not yet valued liquid cooling as a real earnings bridge. That is the upside torque: if data-center cooling fluids move from “concept” to actual orders, the same segment that investors now discount as chemicals can become a specialty AI-infrastructure supplier.

The valuation is cheap on forward-cycle economics, not on stale trailing growth. I model 2026 revenue around CNY 1.45-1.50 billion and net income around CNY 165-175 million, assuming only modest margin improvement. That is EPS of roughly CNY 1.25-1.30, or 21-22x forward earnings. The real base case is 2027: revenue of CNY 1.60-1.70 billion, net margin moving toward 13%, and EPS around CNY 1.60-1.70 as higher-spec oils, excipient GMP share gains, and fixed-cost absorption show through. That puts the stock at roughly 16-17x 2027 earnings, too low for a business with regulated pharma exposure, specialty-materials optionality, CNY 238 million of annual operating cash flow, and a proposed CNY 0.60 per share dividend.

There is also an ownership setup. As of Q1 2026, only four disclosed institutional investors held 11.58% of shares. The shareholder count was only 6,687 at March 31, but liquidity remains small-cap retail-driven. That cuts both ways: weak tape can hurt quickly, but a credible proof point in liquid cooling, injectable excipients, or GMP-driven share gains can move the multiple violently.

The main risks are not theoretical. Excipient pricing is under pressure from centralized drug procurement. Injectable excipient revenue declined in 2025 and was still slightly down year over year in Q1 2026, so the highest-quality pharma mix is not yet inflecting. The controlling shareholder group has share pledges, and an employee holding platform announced a small reduction plan. If the 30,000-ton project adds commodity capacity instead of specialty volumes, the market will be right to keep a chemical multiple on the business.

The stock works because expectations are low. Investors are paying roughly current-cycle value for the existing lube and excipient base, while getting the 2026 GMP industry cleanup, high-end excipient pipeline, POE ester ramp, 30,000-ton project, and data-center cooling optionality for a modest premium.

Using CNY 27.80 as the current price:

Bear case: CNY 20, down 28%. EPS stalls around CNY 1.05-1.15, injectable excipients keep declining, new lube capacity is price-competitive rather than specialty, and the market compresses the stock to 18x trough earnings.

Base case: CNY 42, up 51%. 2027 EPS reaches roughly CNY 1.65, the market values Well at 25x as a specialty excipient and synthetic-materials compounder, and dividend plus cash flow provide patience while the transition becomes visible.

Bull case: CNY 68, up 145%. EPS moves above CNY 2.20 as GMP share gains, injectable recovery, EV and wind oils, and data-center cooling fluids all hit together. The stock gets a 30x multiple as investors stop treating it like a cyclical chemical small-cap.

metadata.company_name: Nanjing Well Pharmaceutical Group Co. Ltd. Class A
